agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"Order cancellation feature for an e-commerce/order management system. Three stakeholders (Product, Finance, Operations) have provided requirements that govern when an order may be cancelled, how invoicing works, and the relationship between the two.","bounded_context":"Order Lifecycle Management — spanning order placement, invoicing, cancellation, and shipment.","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Order","definition":"A customer's request to purchase goods, tracked through a lifecycle from placement to shipment/delivery."},{"term":"Cancellation","definition":"The act of voiding an order, preventing it from being fulfilled and shipped."},{"term":"Invoice","definition":"A financial document generated to lock in pricing and record the obligation to pay. Described by Finance as irreversible once created."},{"term":"Shipment","definition":"The physical dispatch of goods to the customer, representing a point-of-no-return in the order lifecycle."},{"term":"Order Placement","definition":"The initial act of creating an order, which per Operations triggers immediate invoicing."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"An order may be cancelled at any time before it has shipped.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is a business policy defined by the PM governing the cancellation window. A VP could change the window (e.g., only within 1 hour).","testable_assertion":"GIVEN an order in any pre-shipment state, WHEN a cancellation is requested, THEN the cancellation is accepted."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"An invoiced order cannot be cancelled; invoicing is irreversible.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Finance declares invoicing irreversible — this is a financial integrity constraint. Reversing an invoice without a credit note would corrupt the ledger.","testable_assertion":"GIVEN an order that has been invoiced, WHEN a cancellation is requested, THEN the cancellation is rejected."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"Orders are invoiced at the time of placement to lock in pricing.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is an operational policy to lock pricing. The timing of invoicing could be changed by business decision (e.g., invoice at shipment instead).","testable_assertion":"GIVEN a new order is placed, WHEN placement is confirmed, THEN an invoice is immediately generated for that order."}],"acceptance_criteria":[{"id":"AC-1","given":"An order has been placed but not yet shipped","when":"The customer or an agent requests cancellation","then":"The system must determine whether cancellation is permitted based on the resolved business rules (currently contradictory — see escalations)"},{"id":"AC-2","given":"An order has been placed and therefore immediately invoiced (per BR-3)","when":"A cancellation is requested before shipment","then":"The outcome is undefined — BR-1 permits it, BR-2 forbids it"},{"id":"AC-3","given":"An order has shipped","when":"A cancellation is requested","then":"The cancellation is rejected regardless of invoice status"}],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Order placed and immediately cancelled (within seconds)","expected_behavior":"Undefined due to contradiction: the order is already invoiced per BR-3, so BR-2 blocks cancellation, but BR-1 allows it since it hasn't shipped."},{"scenario":"Invoice generation fails or is delayed after placement","expected_behavior":"If invoicing is asynchronous and hasn't completed, a brief window may exist where cancellation is allowed under both BR-1 and BR-2 — but this contradicts BR-3's 'at time of placement' guarantee."},{"scenario":"Partial shipment of a multi-item order","expected_behavior":"Unspecified. Can unshipped items be cancelled? Their invoice status is unclear."},{"scenario":"Credit note or refund after cancellation","expected_behavior":"Finance says invoicing is irreversible, but if cancellation is allowed, the financial reversal mechanism (credit note, void, refund) is unspecified."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"High — if invoiced orders are cancelled without a proper credit/void mechanism, the general ledger becomes inconsistent. If cancellation is blocked entirely, customer satisfaction and chargeback rates will suffer.","operational":"High — Operations invoices at placement to lock pricing. Any resolution that decouples invoicing from placement changes their pricing-lock workflow. Any resolution that allows cancellation of invoiced orders requires a new credit note or void process.","user":"High — customers expect to cancel orders that haven't shipped. If the system silently blocks all cancellations (because every order is invoiced at placement), customer trust erodes and support volume spikes.","risk":"Critical — shipping a contradictory rule set means either (a) cancellation never works and customers escalate, or (b) invoiced orders get cancelled and the financial ledger is corrupted. Both outcomes are severe.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Customers","impact":"Cannot cancel any order if Finance rule wins; poor experience drives chargebacks and churn."},{"group":"Finance Team","impact":"Ledger integrity at risk if PM rule wins and invoiced orders are cancelled without a compensating transaction."},{"group":"Operations Team","impact":"Must redesign invoicing timing if resolution decouples invoice from placement, affecting pricing-lock strategy."},{"group":"Customer Support","impact":"Will bear the brunt of complaints if cancellation is silently blocked for all orders."},{"group":"Product Management","impact":"Must reconcile the cancellation promise with financial constraints — may need to redefine the feature scope."}]},"confidence":{"level":40,"reasoning":"Three explicit stakeholder requirements directly contradict each other in combination. Taken together, BR-1 + BR-2 + BR-3 make cancellation simultaneously always-permitted (pre-ship) and never-permitted (always invoiced). No amount of analysis can resolve this without a stakeholder decision. Confidence is very low because the spec as written is internally inconsistent.","high_confidence_areas":["Each individual rule is clearly stated and unambiguous in isolation","The contradiction is definitive and not a matter of interpretation","Shipped orders cannot be cancelled — all three stakeholders agree on this boundary"],"low_confidence_areas":["Whether Finance would accept a credit-note mechanism as distinct from cancellation","Whether Operations could defer invoicing to a later lifecycle stage","Whether the PM's intent was to override the finance constraint or was unaware of it"],"assumptions":["Invoicing at placement means every placed order is immediately invoiced with no exception","The three statements represent current, active requirements — none are outdated or aspirational"]},"escalations":[{"type":"contradiction","description":"BR-1 (cancel before shipment) directly contradicts BR-2 + BR-3 in combination. Since orders are invoiced at placement (BR-3), and invoiced orders cannot be cancelled (BR-2), no order can ever be cancelled — yet BR-1 says they can be cancelled anytime before shipment. All three rules are explicitly stated by different stakeholders and cannot all hold simultaneously.","affected_stakeholders":["Product Management","Finance Team","Operations Team","Customers","Customer Support"],"options":["Option A: Finance wins — orders cannot be cancelled once invoiced. PM must remove or redefine the cancellation feature (e.g., cancellation becomes a credit-note/refund process, not a true void).","Option B: PM wins — allow cancellation before shipment and introduce a credit note or invoice void mechanism so Finance has a compensating transaction. Finance must accept that 'irreversible' means the invoice is preserved but offset, not that the order is locked.","Option C: Operations adjusts — defer invoicing to a later stage (e.g., at shipment or at pick/pack) so there is a genuine pre-invoice window where cancellation is clean. Operations must find another mechanism to lock pricing."],"recommendation":"Option B is the most common industry pattern. Introduce a credit note or void mechanism so that cancellation before shipment is permitted, the original invoice is preserved for audit, and a compensating financial entry restores ledger integrity. This satisfies the PM's customer-facing requirement, preserves Finance's audit trail, and requires only a process addition (credit notes) rather than a fundamental workflow change. However, all three stakeholders MUST agree — this cannot be decided by engineering."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"The three requirements as stated are mutually exclusive. Implementing any two faithfully violates the third.","business_rule_broken":"BR-1 vs BR-2 + BR-3 — cancellation is both always permitted (pre-ship) and never permitted (always invoiced)."}]}

Three stakeholders (Product, Finance, Operations) have provided requirements that govern when an order may be cancelled, how invoicing works, and the relationship between the two.","bounded_context":"Order Lifecycle Management — spanning order placement, invoicing, cancellation, and shipment.","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Order","definition":"A customer's request to purchase goods, tr

content.thinking:
Let me analyze these three requirements carefully:

1. PM: "An order can be cancelled at any time before it ships."
2. Finance: "Orders that have been invoiced cannot be cancelled — invoicing is irreversible."
3. Operations: "We invoice orders at the time of placement to lock in pricing."

The contradiction is clear:
- If orders are invoiced at placement (Ops), and invoiced orders can't be cancelled (Finance), then orders can NEVER be cancelled (since they're invoiced immediately).
- But the PM says orders CAN be cancelled before shipping.

This is a direct contradiction between explicit stakeholder requirements. All three are stated explicitly and they cannot all be satisfied simultaneously.


I'm flagging this as a genuine contradiction that needs escalation, though I'm not entirely confident in how to resolve it without getting clarification from the stakeholders.

All three rules are explicitly stated by different stakeholders and cannot all hold simultaneously.","affected_stakeholders":["Product Management","Finance Team","Operations Team","Customers","Customer Support"],"options":["Option A: Finance wins — orders cannot be cancelled once invoiced. PM must remove or redefine the cancellation feature (e.g., cancellation becomes a credit-note/refund process, not a true void).","Option B: PM wins — allow cancellation before shipment and introduce a credit note or invoice void mechanism so Finance has a compensating transaction. Finance must accept that 'irreversible' means the invoice is preserved but offset, not that the order is locked.","Option C: Operations adjusts — defer invoicing to a later stage (e.g., at shipment or at pick/pack) so there is a genuine pre-invoice window where cancellation is clean. Introduce a credit note or void mechanism so that cancellation before shipment is permitted, the original invoice is preserved for audit, and a compensating financial entry restores ledger integrity. However, all three stakeholders MUST agree — this cannot be decided by engineering."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"The three requirements as stated are mutually exclusive.
